cards.back: A tiny internal DC error modeled as a battery in series with the input, caused by manufacturing mismatches in the input transistor pair.

cards.front: If V_OS = 2mV, what happens to the output of an inverting amplifier with a gain of -10 when the input is grounded?
cards.back: The V_OS error acts as a non-inverting input. The error is amplified by the non-inverting gain (1 + 10) = 11. Error = 11 * 2mV = 22mV output.

cards.front: What does α (alpha) stand for in op-amp spec sheets?
cards.back: Thermal Drift. It signifies how much the Input Offset Voltage V_OS changes per degree Celsius (ΔV_OS/ΔT).

cards.front: What is the purpose of the 'Offset Null' pins (1 & 5) on a 741?
cards.back: They allow a manual potentiometer to be attached, letting a human technician manually inject currents to perfectly cancel out the manufacturing offset errors.

cards.front: Why is an ideal integrator circuit guaranteed to fail in the real world?
cards.back: Because it will mathematically integrate the tiny internal V_OS error continuously. A constant integral becomes a linear ramp until the op-amp saturates.
The average of the tiny constant DC currents that must physically flow into the (+) and (-) pins to bias the internal transistors into linear operation.
The absolute difference between the two bias currents entering the (+) and (-) pins (|I_B+ - I_B-|).

cards.front: Why can Input Bias Current (I_B) create huge errors in high-impedance circuits?
cards.back: Because that tiny current must flow through your input resistors. If the resistor is massive (e.g., 10MΩ), even tiny currents create massive voltage drops right at the input pin.

cards.front: Why are FET-input op-amps superior to BJT op-amps for extremely delicate, high-impedance sensor circuits?
cards.back: FETs have gate insulation drawing practically zero bias current (picoamps) compared to BJTs (nanoamps/microamps) that require constant base current.

cards.front: When performing the Null Compensation procedure on a 741, what state must the two input pins be in?
cards.back: Both the Inverting and Non-Inverting inputs MUST be shorted directly to Ground (0V) to ensure you are only neutralizing the internal error.
